---
title: Dovetail joinery by hand
date: 2024-02-11
tags: [woodworking, joinery, hand-tools]
---

Dovetails are the signature joint of a well-made drawer. The angled pins and tails resist pulling in the direction a drawer is opened thousands of times, and when cut well they need almost no glue to stay together for a century.

I cut tails first. A sharp marking gauge scribes a line at the thickness of the mating board, and a dovetail saw held just outside the line leaves a surface a few shavings proud of the final fit. I chop the waste out with a bench chisel against a backing board to keep the grain from blowing out.

Transferring the tails to the pin board is the moment of truth. A knife tracing the tails gives a reference line, and careful sawing on the waste side of that line yields a joint that taps together with hand pressure alone. Everything after that is practice.
